Actinic keratoses.description: Precancerous skin growth caused by sun damage.
Actinic keratoses.recommendations: Avoid direct sun exposure, wear protective clothing, and use broad-spectrum sunscreen. Monitor for changes in size, color, or shape, and consult a dermatologist regularly.
Actinic keratoses.medications: Topical treatments (e.g., fluorouracil, imiquimod), cryotherapy, or photodynamic therapy as prescribed by a doctor.
Actinic keratoses.diet_plan: Consume an antioxidant-rich diet with plenty of fruits, vegetables, and omega-3 fatty acids to support skin repair.

Basal cell carcinoma.description: A type of skin cancer that most often develops on areas of skin exposed to the sun.
Basal cell carcinoma.recommendations: Immediate medical evaluation is necessary. Avoid sun exposure and use high SPF sunscreen daily.
Basal cell carcinoma.medications: Surgical removal, topical treatments for superficial cases (imiquimod, 5-fluorouracil), or radiation therapy under medical supervision.
Basal cell carcinoma.diet_plan: A diet high in vitamins A, C, and E, and zinc to promote immune function and skin healing.

Benign keratosis.description: A common, non-cancerous skin growth that usually appears as a brown, black or light tan growth.
Benign keratosis.recommendations: Usually harmless and doesn't require treatment unless it is irritated or for cosmetic reasons. Use moisturizer to prevent dryness and itching.
Benign keratosis.medications: Over-the-counter moisturizers with alpha-hydroxy acids or urea can help soften the lesions. Medical removal (cryosurgery, electrocautery) if necessary.
Benign keratosis.diet_plan: Maintain a balanced, healthy diet to support overall skin health. Ensure adequate hydration.

Dermatofibroma.description: Common noncancerous skin growths that are firm and often appear on the lower legs.
Dermatofibroma.recommendations: Generally require no treatment unless they are painful, itchy, or cosmetically undesired. Avoid scratching or picking.
Dermatofibroma.medications: No specific medications required unless symptomatic. Corticosteroid injections can flatten them, or surgical excision if removal is desired.
Dermatofibroma.diet_plan: Anti-inflammatory diet to lower overall systemic inflammation, focusing on fresh produce, nuts, and fish.

Melanocytic nevi.description: Commonly known as moles, these are usually noncancerous growths of pigment cells.
Melanocytic nevi.recommendations: Perform monthly self-exams using the ABCDE guidelines (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolving). See a dermatologist if any mole changes or bleeds.
Melanocytic nevi.medications: Not applicable unless they become atypical or malignant, at which point biopsy and surgical removal are considered.
Melanocytic nevi.diet_plan: A standard balanced diet is sufficient. Sun protection is key.

Melanoma.description: The most serious type of skin cancer, develops in the cells that produce melanin.
Melanoma.recommendations: Urgent consultation with an oncologist and dermatologist. Avoid all deliberate sun exposure and tanning beds. Regular, thorough skin checks.
Melanoma.medications: Depending on the stage, treatments include surgical excision, immunotherapy (e.g., Keytruda, Opdivo), targeted therapy, or chemotherapy.
Melanoma.diet_plan: A highly nutritious diet supporting the immune system during treatment. Consult a clinical dietitian for personalized oncology nutrition.

Vascular lesions.description: Arise from malformed blood vessels and include conditions like cherry angiomas or spider veins.
Vascular lesions.recommendations: Most are benign. Protect skin from injury and harsh sunlight. If bleeding occurs or for cosmetic reasons, consult a specialist.
Vascular lesions.medications: Laser therapy or intense pulsed light (IPL) are common treatments. In some cases, sclerotherapy is used.
Vascular lesions.diet_plan: A diet rich in bioflavonoids and vitamin C, such as citrus fruits, berries, and leafy greens, may help strengthen blood vessel walls.
